description: Head-to-head: line-Jaccard vs cursor-agent (composer-2.5) read-only judge on a stratified sample of pairs. Ground truth: test-outcome Jaccard ≥ 0.999 (verifier sees both trials identically).

pairs.agent_reasoning: Both patches apply the same production fix in sqlite3/schema.py: add_field now includes `or field.unique` so unique columns (including nullable OneToOneField) use _remake_table instead of ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN. The only differences are comment wording and an extra regression test in A, which do not change runtime behavior.

Both patches make the same one-line fix in _log_reg_scoring_path: pass multi_class into the temporary LogisticRegression used for CV scoring. The only difference is keyword argument order, which is semantically irrelevant in Python. No other files or logic differ between A and B.

Both patches fix the same bug by passing multi_class into the temporary LogisticRegression inside _log_reg_scoring_path, so multinomial LogisticRegressionCV scoring uses softmax probabilities instead of OvR. The only library code difference is that A also forwards random_state to that helper instance, which is never used because fit is not called before scoring. Extra ad-hoc test scripts in A do not change sklearn runtime behavior.

Both patches expose transform_max_iter and forward it to sparse_encode in transform(), preserving default behavior at 1000. They differ only in where the parameter sits in __init__/_set_sparse_coding_params and in docstrings added by B; positional call sites after transform_alpha could diverge, but keyword-based usage and transform outputs align.

Both patches add the same guard in ConditionSet._eval_subs when substitution yields cond is S.true. Patch A routes through ConditionSet(sym, S.true, base), which __new__ immediately reduces to base_set; patch B returns base directly. That is the only library change and matches the issue-19495 fix, so observable substitution behavior is the same on all inputs where the condition is exactly S.true.

Both patches delete the same erroneous `if cond is S.true` early return in `ConditionSet._eval_subs`, leaving byte-identical production code. Patch B only updates a test assertion to match the corrected substitution semantics; runtime behavior is the same on all inputs.

Both patches add OutputWrapper.flush() that delegates to self._out, fixing migrate and other commands that call self.stdout.flush() on real streams. The only behavioral difference is defensive: B skips flush when _out has no flush method (like isatty), while A always calls _out.flush() and can raise AttributeError. For normal management-command I/O this is indistinguishable.

Both patches set the same default ('same-origin') in global_settings and settings docs, so default deployments get identical Referrer-Policy headers and check behavior. SecurityMiddleware and check_referrer_policy logic are unchanged between A and B. Patch A only adds updated W022 warning/docs text for the explicit-None case; B keeps the old 'not set' message, which diverges only in deploy-check output strings, not HTTP or middleware behavior.

Both skip Value() wrapping for F(), combined F expressions, and Func-based expressions, fixing the same bulk_update bug. The sole logic difference is expression detection: isinstance(Expression, F) versus hasattr(resolve_expression), which only diverges for rare BaseExpression types like Subquery. Test changes are cosmetic variants of the same F() scenarios.

Both patches add the same _print_Indexed and _print_IndexedBase methods to PythonCodePrinter in pycode.py. The sole difference is syntactic: tuple unpacking (base, *index = expr.args) versus explicit slicing (args[0] and args[1:]), which are equivalent for Indexed's required args layout. No other files differ, so observable pycode/lambdify output is the same on all valid inputs.

Both patches apply the same fix to _ensure_numeric in polyval: split datetime and timedelta handling and use np.timedelta64(0) as the offset for timedelta data instead of a datetime64 epoch. The only difference is if versus elif for the timedelta branch, which cannot change behavior because the datetime branch returns first. No other files differ between A and B.

Both patches apply the same django-11276 fix—switching escape() to html.escape in django/utils/html.py—with identical code everywhere except urlize()'s unescape() apostrophe mapping. Patch A still reverses ' (legacy cgi.escape entity) while patch B reverses ' (what html.escape emits), so they agree on almost all inputs but diverge when urlize processes HTML-escaped apostrophes in URL-like text.

Both patches add the identical single line in Collector.delete()'s fast-delete path to set the instance PK to None after delete_batch, matching the normal delete path at lines 325-327. Working trees are byte-identical; only git commit metadata differs.

Patches A and B are byte-identical: each adds only kwargs.setdefault("disabled", True) in ReadOnlyPasswordHashField.__init__, leaving bound_data, has_changed, and clean_password unchanged. The A/ and B/ working trees match, so every input sees the same form validation, cleaning, and rendering behavior.

Both patches fix polyval for datetime-like coordinates, but A only adjusts _ensure_numeric (correct timedelta offset) while keeping the original Horner loop with zeros_like; B restructures polyval to use get_clean_interp_index for named coords, removes zeros_like, and transposes output dimensions. B never adds A’s timedelta-specific offset fix, so timedelta and several coord/naming paths can disagree, and B’s dim reordering changes observable structure even when values match.

Both patches address the same IndexError when split_surds has no Pow surds, but at different layers. A catches the error only in _sqrt_match and falls back to depth-based matching; B makes split_surds return (1, 0, expr), which changes _sqrt_match to return [expr, 0, 1] instead of using the fallback. On the FAIL_TO_PASS case sqrtdenest((3 - sqrt(2)*sqrt(4 + 3*I) + 3*I)/2), A resolves to I while B does not, so they diverge on inputs users care about despite overlapping intent.

Both patches change only SpanSelector._setup_edge_handle to stop initializing ToolLineHandles from self.extents (0, 0), fixing the axes-autoscale bug when limits exclude zero. Patch A uses axis limit endpoints; patch B uses 10%/20% interior points, so initial handle positions differ until extents are set, but typical draw-and-drag workflows converge once a span exists.

Both patches only change Q.deconstruct() and add overlapping tests, targeting the same Exists/single-child edge case. B preserves the base behavior of emitting a lone keyword lookup in kwargs (with a tuple guard), while A always serializes children into args, so simple Q(foo=bar) and negated forms return different (args, kwargs) pairs and migration strings. ORM filtering after reconstruction is typically equivalent, but the deconstruct API output diverges on the most common inputs.

Both patches fix parent-link selection when multiple OneToOneFields target the same parent, but A only indexes explicit parent_link=True fields while B still treats a sole non-parent_link OneToOneField as the inheritance link. That difference changes model setup and check() for single-field MTI children, so they are not behaviorally equivalent on the benchmark’s required cases even though they agree on the multi-field ordering scenario.

Both patches identically add violation_error_code to CheckConstraint and UniqueConstraint __init__, validate (ValidationError code), and __eq__, reusing BaseConstraint deconstruct/clone. The sole runtime difference is that A includes violation_error_code in __repr__ while B omits it; A also adds tests but does not change validation paths. For model validation and migrations, behavior matches; only repr/debug output differs when a custom code is set.

Both address sqrtdenest failures when split_surds finds no Pow surds (e.g. complex I terms), but A only guards _sqrt_match to return [] when b is zero while B changes split_surds to return (1, 0, expr), yielding different _sqrt_match results and denesting paths. Their tests also disagree: A leaves sqrt(3+I) unchanged whereas B expects the issue expression to simplify to I.

Both patches replace the same hardcoded year<70 logic in parse_http_date with an identical sliding-century algorithm using utcnow and a 50-year window. Century addition is mathematically the same; the only behavioral difference is > versus >= on the 50-year check, which disagrees for exactly one two-digit year per current year (e.g. 76 when current_year is 2026). All other tested and typical HTTP date inputs parse the same.

Both patches make the same change to k_means(): pre-generate per-init seeds once, use them in the n_jobs==1 loop, and remove the duplicate seeds line in the parallel branch. The git diffs against base are identical and the A/ and B/ trees match, so they behave the same on all inputs; only the edit sequence differs.

Both patches replace the hardcoded 70/1900–2000 century split in parse_http_date with the same RFC 7231 sliding 50-year window logic, differing only in whether the anchor year is taken from utcnow() (A) or now() (B). That one-line choice is behaviorally equivalent in many common cases but diverges for two-digit years when local and UTC years differ or when tests pin utcnow to 2019/2020/2048 while B still reads the real local year. A matches the gold fix and intended GMT-relative behavior; B is the same fix idea with a wrong clock reference.

Both patches replace the same two-digit-year logic in parse_http_date with the same RFC 7231 sliding 50-year window, but A anchors on utcnow() and a computed century while B uses now() and a hardcoded +2000. That matches on most current-era HTTP dates yet diverges on the fail-to-pass suite (which mocks utcnow), at timezone/year boundaries, and after 2099 when dynamic vs fixed century matters.

Both patches prevent the IndexError when split_surds has no Pow surds by returning (1, 0, expr), so _sqrt_match and sqrtdenest behave the same on that path and both fail test_issue_12420 identically. Patch A guards any empty _split_gcd call while patch B only short-circuits the initial empty-surds case; they can diverge if surds is non-empty but a recursive _split_gcd(*b1n) receives an empty list after factoring.

Both patches apply the same one-line semantic change in ModelBase.__new__: when collecting parent_links, only overwrite an existing entry for the same parent if the new OneToOneField has parent_link=True. The A/B diff in base.py is limited to comments and the local name key vs related_tuple. Tests differ in file layout and breadth but assert the same parent-link selection behavior.

Both patches apply the identical one-line condition in ModelBase parent_links collection: only store/overwrite when the key is new or the field has parent_link=True. The only differences are comment wording, test style/coverage, and A's extra standalone debug scripts that are not part of the framework. On all MTI model definitions, parent link selection behavior is the same.

Both patches fix the same bug by serializing and deserializing ExceptionChainRepr.chain with the same JSON structure and restoring ExceptionChainRepr instead of ReprExceptionInfo. Refactoring differs (tuple vs dict helpers, import placement) but the happy path for chained failures with tracebacks is the same. They diverge on edge cases: B handles None reprcrash in a chain and copies dicts before deserialize mutation, while A can AttributeError on serialize or break on reuse of the same report dict.

Both patches apply the identical change to sklearn/cluster/k_means_.py: pre-generate n_init integer seeds once, iterate for seed in seeds in the serial path with random_state=seed, and reuse the same seeds in the parallel branch. A/ and B/ trees are byte-identical in the patched file, fixing the bug where n_jobs=1 reused one RNG state per init while n_jobs>1 used distinct seeds.

Both patches apply the same conditional in django/db/models/base.py when collecting parent_links for MTI: keep the first OneToOneField per parent unless a later field has parent_link=True. That fixes field-order-dependent parent link selection with identical runtime semantics. Remaining differences are comments, variable names, test placement, and A’s extra root-level debug scripts, none of which affect framework behavior on user inputs.

Both patches only change the CDS parser’s division grammar to support left-associative slash chains, which is what astropy-14369 needs for strings like 10+3J/m/s/kpc2. For slash-only compound units the middle rule is effectively the same because a single unit is both a unit_expression and a product_of_units. They diverge on edge cases where the denominator is a dotted product (e.g. J/m.s), which B’s product_of_units rule handles but A’s unit_expression-on-both-sides rule does not.

Both patches change EnumSerializer from value-based constructor calls to member-name attribute references, producing the same migration strings and imports for all enum members. The only code differences are stylistic (local variable vs inline self.value.name) and non-runtime test scaffolding.

Both patches fix chained-exception report serialization by adding a chain array to the JSON and restoring ExceptionChainRepr on deserialize instead of ReprExceptionInfo. Structure and behavior match for typical chained failures with tracebacks. They diverge when a chain link has reprcrash=None: B serializes/deserializes that safely while A calls .__dict__ on None and fails.

Both patches replace the same hardcoded year<70 logic in parse_http_date with the RFC 7231 sliding 50-year window using now().year. Patch A adds the current century then subtracts 100 when year - current_year > 50; patch B chooses current vs previous century with the algebraically identical test year - (current_year % 100) > 50. No other files differ, so every HTTP date input maps to the same parsed timestamp.

Both patches fix the same bug by changing ConciseDateFormatter's show_offset suppression from level<2 (years and months) to year-level only. Patch A uses `level == 0` and patch B uses `level < 1`, which are equivalent for the integer level values 0–5 produced by the loop. The accompanying test updates are identical between the two trees.

Both patches replace the same hardcoded year<70 branch in parse_http_date with identical RFC 7231 sliding-window logic using now().year and the same century adjustment. The sole textual difference is year > current_year + 50 versus year - current_year > 50, which are algebraically the same for integer years. No other files differ, so every HTTP date input yields the same parsed timestamp.

Both patches apply the identical two-line fix in Dataset.swap_dims: after to_index_variable(), if var is v they shallow-copy before setting var.dims, preventing in-place mutation of the source dataset's IndexVariable. The trial diffs and resulting A/B trees are the same; only commit metadata differs.

Patches A and B apply the same one-line logic change (suppress offset only for year-level ticks, not months) and the same test expectation update. The applied trees are identical in the two modified files, so runtime behavior matches on all inputs.

Both patches only change p_product_of_units in cds.py to correctly distinguish PRODUCT ('.') from DIVISION ('/') when len(p)==4, with identical multiply, divide, and unary-inverse semantics. PLY stores lexeme strings as token values, so A's p[2]=='.' branch and B's p[2]=='/' branch are logically complementary and equivalent for all grammar cases. No other files differ between A and B.

Both patches fix the same bug in Add._eval_is_zero by replacing the boolean im flag with a list of imaginary terms and, when real parts cancel, checking whether the imaginary parts cancel instead of always returning False. The sole semantic difference is whether cancellation is tested on a/I (A) or on the raw imaginary terms (B); for is_imaginary args these are equivalent in principle but may differ slightly on unevaluated symbolic sums due to different simplification paths.
